ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for tavuk sote; closest_parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002725) used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Chicken meat, cooked) used as closest_parent; no specific code for sautéed chicken with tomato-based sauce exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for tavuk sote as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of tavuk sote is approximately 300 g, comprising diced sautéed chicken with vegetables in tomato-based sauce, typically served with butter rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stewed — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA data for tomato sauce, onion, and capsicum components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Tavuk sote is a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken (NDB 05064), tomato sauce (NDB 11549), onion (NDB 11282), and capsicum (NDB 11333), weighted to approximate a typical recipe composition: ~65% chicken, ~15% tomato sauce, ~10% onion, ~10% capsicum. Values represent the cooked dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries for mixed chicken and vegetable dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tavuk sote. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed chicken-and-vegetable dishes with tomato-based sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with modest carbohydrate from tomato sauce, onion, and capsicum; these low-GI vegetables and the protein-dominant matrix support a low GI estimate. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 14.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 300 g restaurant serving of tavuk sote contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation and avoid adding extra salt or high-sodium condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~4.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tavuk sote has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 and a glycemic load of about 6.5 per standard serving, making it a suitable protein-rich main dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served with low-GI accompaniments such as bulgur or salad rather than white rice or bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean poultry protein with moderate saturated fat (~1.6 g per 100 g) and beneficial vegetables
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Tavuk sote provides lean chicken protein with relatively low saturated fat and includes capsicum and tomato sauce rich in antioxidants (vitamin C, lycopene); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose skinless chicken and oil-minimal preparation to keep saturated fat intake within AHA guidelines of less than 13 g per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tavuk sote (Turkish: tavuk = chicken, sote = sauté) is a classic Turkish and broader Middle Eastern cooked chicken dish in which diced or bite-sized pieces of chicken are sautéed in oil over high heat with sliced onions and capsicum (bell pepper), then simmered in a homemade tomato-based sauce seasoned with salt, black pepper, and paprika. The result is a tender, juicy chicken dish with a mildly tangy, savoury tomato sauce and soft caramelised vegetables. The texture is moist and the flavour profile is aromatic and mildly spiced. In Singapore, tavuk sote is served at Middle Eastern and Turkish restaurants such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant and is typically accompanied by butter rice or flatbread. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 142 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 6.2 g fat (of which 1.6 g saturated), and 4.8 g carbohydrate, making it a protein-dense, moderate-calorie main course. The low glycemic index (estimated ~45) and modest carbohydrate content make it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high at approximately 310 mg per 100 g, which is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal, and is rich in niacin, vitamin B6, phosphorus, and selenium from the chicken component, with additional vitamin C and antioxidants from the capsicum and tomato sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in tavuk sote?
Is tavuk sote good for diabetics?
What is sautéed chicken Turkish style?
Middle Eastern chicken dish with tomato sauce
meal_context: served with butter rice or flatbread

confidence_reason: Tavuk sote is not present as a named entry in any primary regional approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked chicken, tomato sauce, onion, and capsicum, weighted by typical recipe proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar mixed chicken-vegetable dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-weighted estimates from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked chicken ~65%, tomato sauce ~15%, onion ~10%, capsicum ~10%); no exact tavuk sote entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries used for this composite estimate. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on USDA SR Legacy data for the component ingredients — no hydrogenated fats are used in standard tavuk sote preparation. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; direct GI measurement for tavuk sote has not been published in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 5. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may be lower. 6. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant main-course portion; actual restaurant portions may vary. 7. The dish is classified as halal based on the use of chicken and vegetable ingredients with no pork or alcohol in standard preparation; individual restaurant practices should be verified. 8. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 9. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi reflect the Singapore serving context of this dish as recorded on field survey data.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (cooked chicken, tomato sauce, onion, capsicum) with recipe-proportion weighting.
Estimated GI at 45 (low) using University of Sydney GI Database analogous mixed chicken-vegetable dish entries; documented estimation basis in gi_source.
Added multilingual local_names for Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 and Q1 for Singapore serving context.
Populated aliases_by_language and verified all entries present verbatim in flat aliases list to satisfy Q8 consistency rule.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 in notes as not reported in source database to satisfy G3 sentinel value rule.
Confirmed trans_fat_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy component data; documented in notes.
Set ontology match_type values exclusively to allowed values (exact, closest_parent, none); used closest_parent for FoodOn and FoodEx2 with gap notes; none for SNOMED CT.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions with named clinical factors.
